title: Podman doesn't start rootlessport - Fedora 37
body:
### Issue Description

When running rootless container with `-p 8080:8080` it won't enable the port on tcp4, just tcp6. 

~~~
...
DEBU[0000] slirp4netns command: /usr/bin/slirp4netns --disable-host-loopback --mtu=65520 --enable-sandbox --enable-seccomp --enable-ipv6 -c -e 3 -r 4 --netns-type=path /run/user/1001/netns/netns-dca8c436-dc9a-9573-bfe4-c2a0780d86e1 tap0 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg="Starting parent driver" 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg="opaque=map[builtin.readypipepath:/run/user/1001/libpod/tmp/rootlessport3501550536/.bp-ready.pipe builtin.socketpath:/run/user/1001/libpod/tmp/rootlessport3501550536/.bp.sock]" 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg="Starting child driver in child netns (\"/proc/self/exe\" [rootlessport-child])" 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg="Waiting for initComplete" 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg="initComplete is closed; parent and child established the communication channel" 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg="Exposing ports [{ 8080 8080 1 tcp}]" 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport: time="2023-04-03T20:30:36-07:00" level=info msg=Ready 
DEBU[0000] rootlessport is ready                        
DEBU[0000] found local resolver, using "/run/systemd/resolve/resolv.conf" to get the nameservers 
...

# sudo netstat -plan | grep 8080
tcp6       0      0 :::8080                 :::*                    LISTEN      3274/rootlessport
~~~

~~~
# podman version

Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.4.2
API Version:  4.4.2
Go Version:   go1.19.6
Built:        Wed Mar  1 03:22:59 2023
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
~~~

comments.body:
Did you actually test that? rootlessport binds the dual stack address by default so you can use it via ipv4 and ipv6.

netstat is deprecated for a long time, it doesn't display these dual stack binds correctly, use `ss` instead:
```
$ ss -tulpn | grep 8080
tcp   LISTEN 0      4096                *:8080             *:*    users:(("rootlessport",pid=29344,fd=11))
$ netstat -tulpn | grep 8080
(Not all processes could be identified, non-owned process info
 will not be shown, you would have to be root to see it all.)
tcp6       0      0 :::8080                 :::*                    LISTEN      29344/rootlessport
```

> Did you actually test that? rootlessport binds the dual stack address by default so you can use it via ipv4 and ipv6.
> 
> netstat is deprecated for a long time, it doesn't display these dual stack binds correctly, use `ss` instead:
> 
> ```
> $ ss -tulpn | grep 8080
> tcp   LISTEN 0      4096                *:8080             *:*    users:(("rootlessport",pid=29344,fd=11))
> $ netstat -tulpn | grep 8080
> (Not all processes could be identified, non-owned process info
>  will not be shown, you would have to be root to see it all.)
> tcp6       0      0 :::8080                 :::*                    LISTEN      29344/rootlessport
> ```

LoL, yeah feeling strange really. I've tried the ss as well, but it doesn't show it listens on tcp4, only tcp6.

However, I tested it on application that really did not bound to the port - dummy process in container. 
Testing it with python http server it works as it should.
So yeah, it works. Thx for looking at it.
